claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 2 children:
  - Alex is 12 years old and does not live with you.
  - Blake is 6 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 12 (under 16)
  - Child does not live with claimant and weekly upkeep (£20.00) is below the Child Benefit rate (£26.05)
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child in hospital for 12 weeks (within 12-week limit)
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

Regarding Blake:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 6 (under 16)
  - Another claimant who lives with the child has priority
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is fostered and the council pays for their accommodation or maintenance
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Child does not live with claimant and weekly upkeep (£20.00) is below the Child Benefit rate (£26.05)
circumstances: Child does not live with claimant and weekly upkeep (£20.00) is below the Child Benefit rate (£26.05)
Child is not in local authority care
Child in hospital for 12 weeks (within 12-week limit)
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Hello! I'd be happy to help you find out if you're eligible for Child Benefit.

To assess your eligibility, I'll need to ask you a few questions about your circumstances.

Let's start: **How many children are you enquiring about, and what are their names and ages?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hello! I'm interested in finding out if I'm eligible for Child Benefit. I have two children I'd like to ask about - Alex, who's 12, and Blake, who's 6.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information! It's great to meet you, and I'd be happy to help assess your eligibility for Child Benefit for both Alex (12) and Blake (6).

Let me ask you a few questions about your circumstances.

**Are you responsible for both Alex and Blake, and do they both live with you?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Well, Blake lives with me, but Alex doesn't live with me - Alex lives with their other parent. I do help out with some money for Alex though.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for clarifying that. That's helpful information.

Since Alex lives with their other parent, I have a follow-up question about Alex:

**Do you contribute towards the cost of looking after Alex at least the same amount as the current Child Benefit rate (which is £26.05 per week for the eldest child)? And does the parent Alex lives with already claim Child Benefit for Alex?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, I don't contribute that much - I only give about £20 a week to help with things like clothes and bits and bobs for Alex. And yes, their other parent does already claim Child Benefit for Alex.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. That helps clarify Alex's situation.

Now let me ask a couple more questions about your overall circumstances:

**Are you aged 16 or over?**

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great, thank you. 

**Do you live in the UK?** (This includes England, Scotland, Wales, and Northern Ireland.)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I live in the UK - I'm in Manchester.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Lovely, thank you.

Now a question about Blake, who lives with you:

**Is Blake in full-time education, or if not, are they registered with a careers service or similar? Also, is Blake a British citizen or do they have the right to live in the UK?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Blake is only 6, so he's just in primary school - regular school for his age. And yes, he's British.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Perfect, thank you for confirming that. Just one more question:

**Is anyone else currently claiming Child Benefit for Blake, or has Blake been in local authority care for more than 8 weeks?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Actually, yes - Blake is being fostered. I'm fostering him, and the local authority gives me maintenance for him.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for letting me know that Blake is being fostered and you receive maintenance from the local authority for him.

I just need to clarify one more thing about this:

**Has Blake been in local authority care (i.e., being looked after by the local authority, including through fostering arrangements) continuously for more than 8 weeks?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, it's been longer than that - it's been a few months now that Blake has been in this fostering arrangement with me.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for your patience. I now have all the information I need to assess your eligibility for Child Benefit for both Alex and Blake.

Based on what you've told me and the official eligibility rules, let me provide my assessment:

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for answering all my questions. I've now completed my assessment of your eligibility for Child Benefit.

## My Assessment

Unfortunately, **you are not eligible for Child Benefit for either Alex or Blake**. Here's why:

### Alex (age 12)
**Not eligible.** Alex does not live with you, so to qualify you would need to contribute at least the same amount as the Child Benefit rate (£26.05 per week) towards Alex's upkeep. Since you contribute approximately £20 per week, this is below the required threshold. Additionally, Alex's other parent already claims Child Benefit for Alex, and only one person can receive Child Benefit for the same child.

### Blake (age 6)
**Not eligible.** While foster carers can sometimes claim Child Benefit, the rules state that you can only get Child Benefit for a foster child if the local council is **not** paying anything towards their accommodation or maintenance. Since you receive maintenance payments from the local authority for Blake's care, this means you cannot also claim Child Benefit for him.

---

I'm sorry this isn't the news you were hoping for. If your circumstances change in the future (for example, if you increase your contributions for Alex to at least £26.05 per week and their other parent stops claiming), you may wish to reassess your eligibility. If you have any questions, you can contact the Child Benefit Office for further guidance.

reasonings: For Alex: You are not eligible for Child Benefit because Alex does not live with you, and you contribute only £20 per week towards Alex's upkeep. To qualify for Child Benefit for a child who lives with someone else, you must contribute at least the same amount as the Child Benefit rate (currently £26.05 per week for the eldest child). Since your contribution of £20 per week is less than this threshold, you do not meet the requirement. Additionally, Alex's other parent already claims Child Benefit for Alex, and only one person can claim Child Benefit for the same child.

For Blake: You are not eligible for Child Benefit because Blake is a foster child and the local authority is paying you maintenance for his care. According to the rules, you can only get Child Benefit for a foster child 'as long as the local council is not paying anything towards their accommodation or maintenance.' Since you've confirmed that you receive maintenance from the local authority for Blake's care, this disqualifies you from claiming Child Benefit for him.
arguments.overall_reasoning: Unfortunately, you are not eligible for Child Benefit for either child. For Alex, you don't meet the minimum contribution threshold required when a child lives elsewhere, and their other parent already claims for them. For Blake, as a foster carer receiving maintenance payments from the local authority, you cannot also claim Child Benefit for him under the fostering rules.

For Blake, as a foster carer receiving maintenance payments from the local authority, you cannot also claim Child Benefit for him under the fostering rules.
